name: Castillo San Felipe del Morro
shortDescription: The great headland fort of San Juan — key to the Spanish Caribbean empire.
description: El Morro stands on a rocky promontory at the entrance to San Juan Bay, its six-level walls rising 43 metres above the sea. Built by the Spanish beginning in 1539 to defend their Caribbean hub, it withstood attacks by Sir Francis Drake (1595), the Earl of Cumberland (1598), and the Dutch (1625). Together with the adjacent Castillo San Cristóbal, it forms the largest fortification built by the Spanish in the New World.

name: Fort Sumter
shortDescription: Where the American Civil War began — a brick star fort in Charleston harbour.
description: Fort Sumter gained its place in history on April 12, 1861, when Confederate forces opened fire on it — firing the first shots of the American Civil War. Built between 1829 and 1860 on an artificial island at the entrance to Charleston harbour, it was designed as part of the Third System of American coastal defences. Major Robert Anderson's 85-man garrison surrendered after 34 hours; the fort was bombarded throughout the war.

name: Fortaleza de San Carlos de la Cabaña
shortDescription: The largest Spanish colonial fortress in the Americas, guarding Havana Bay.
description: La Cabaña is the largest fortress ever built in the Americas, extending 700 metres along the ridge overlooking Havana Bay. Constructed by Spain after Britain seized Havana in 1762, it was designed to prevent any future capture of the city. King Carlos III of Spain reputedly looked for it with a telescope when told its cost, saying at such a price it must be visible from Madrid. Every evening at 9pm, the cannon is still fired from its walls.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of Old Havana)

name: Christiansted National Historic Site
shortDescription: A well-preserved Danish colonial fort on St. Croix, heart of Danish Caribbean trade.
description: Fort Christiansværn at Christiansted was built by the Danish West India and Guinea Company in 1749 to protect the most prosperous town in the Danish Caribbean. The yellow fort, with its moat and cannon platforms, controlled the main harbour of St. Croix. The surrounding historic district preserves 18th-century Danish colonial architecture, arcaded buildings, and the scale house where enslaved people were sold.

name: Fort Pickens
shortDescription: A Gulf Coast brick fort that stayed in Union hands throughout the Civil War.
description: Fort Pickens was one of the few southern forts that remained in Union hands after secession. Built on Santa Rosa Island at the entrance to Pensacola Bay between 1829–1834, its irregular pentagonal design and thick walls made it formidable. Confederate forces besieged it for months in 1861 without success. The fort is also known for imprisoning Geronimo and other Apache leaders from 1886–1888.

name: Fort San Diego
shortDescription: A star fort protecting the terminus of the Manila Galleon trade route.
description: Fort San Diego was built in 1616 to protect Acapulco — the Pacific terminus of the legendary Manila Galleon trade route that connected Asia, the Americas, and Europe. The original earthwork fort was destroyed by a 1776 earthquake; the current pentagonal star fort was rebuilt immediately after. For 250 years, the annual Manila Galleon arrived here carrying silks, porcelain, and spices exchanged for Mexican silver.

name: Montezuma Castle
shortDescription: A five-story Sinagua cliff dwelling built into a 90-foot limestone alcove.
description: Montezuma Castle is a remarkably well-preserved cliff dwelling of the Sinagua people, built into a 90-foot (27 m) natural limestone alcove in the Verde Valley of Arizona. Despite its name, it has no connection to the Aztec emperor Montezuma — the misnomer was given by 19th-century European settlers. Its five stories contain 20 rooms that sheltered up to 50 people who farmed the valley below.

name: Walls of Cartagena
shortDescription: The most complete surviving colonial walled city in the Americas.
description: After English pirate Francis Drake sacked Cartagena de Indias in 1586, Spain built one of the most elaborate defensive systems in the New World around it. The 11-km circuit of walls — up to 17 metres high and 17 metres thick — took over 200 years to complete. Together with the massive Castillo San Felipe de Barajas, Cartagena's fortifications represent the zenith of Spanish colonial military engineering in the Americas.

name: Tulum
shortDescription: A Maya coastal fortress on clifftops above the Caribbean Sea.
description: Tulum was one of the last cities built and inhabited by the Maya and served as a major trading port. Uniquely, it was enclosed on three sides by walls (on a peninsula, with the fourth side being the sea cliff) — making it one of the few Maya sites with defensive fortifications. The El Castillo pyramid served as a lighthouse, guiding canoes through the reef. Spanish conquistadors first sighted it from ships offshore, marvelling at its scale.

name: Machu Picchu
shortDescription: The lost city of the Incas — a royal estate and sacred site hidden in the Andes clouds.
description: Built around 1450 at the height of the Inca Empire, Machu Picchu was a royal estate of the emperor Pachacuti and possibly a sacred pilgrimage site. Situated at 2,430 metres on a ridge between two peaks above the Urubamba River, it was abandoned during the Spanish conquest and unknown to the outside world until Hiram Bingham brought it to international attention in 1911. Its terraces, temples, and citadel walls showcase masterful Inca stone construction.
